aliases: Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG, Alfabeto, Cantina Valpantena, 2015

english: Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG, Alfabeto, Cantina Valpantena, 2015

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Amarone wine is 150 ml (approximately 150 g), served in a large-bowled red wine glass to allow the complex aromas to develop.

nutrient_source.title: Wine, table, red
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data for Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG is adapted from generic red table wine (USDA SR Legacy Food ID: 11628) as no specific producer- or vintage-level data is available in approved sources. Amarone is a dry red wine produced by the apassimento method, which concentrates sugars and alcohol relative to standard table wine; energy and alcohol content may be slightly higher in practice, but the SR Legacy entry is the closest approved analogue.

gi_source.title: Red wine — glycemic index reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: Red wine is reported as GI effectively zero in the University of Sydney GI Database because its fermentable carbohydrate content is negligible. Per Veritas sentinel rules, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive integer) and gi_category is set to low. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate per 150 g serving is approximately 3.9 g and GI is effectively zero.

hypertension.guidance: Regular or heavy alcohol consumption is an established risk factor for elevated blood pressure; individuals with hypertension should limit Amarone to no more than one standard drink per occasion and seek medical advice before including it in their diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low residual sugar and negligible glycemic index, offset by alcohol-mediated hypoglycaemia risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Amarone della Valpolicella is a dry red wine with very low residual sugar and a negligible glycemic index, but alcohol can potentiate hypoglycaemia in patients on insulin or sulfonylureas; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in strict moderation, always with food, and monitor blood glucose closely.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Moderate red wine consumption has been associated with modest cardiovascular benefit in observational studies, attributed partly to polyphenols such as resveratrol; however, the net cardiovascular risk of alcohol is dose-dependent and individuals with established cardiovascular disease should consult their cardiologist before consuming Amarone regularly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG is one of Italy's most prestigious dry red wines, produced exclusively in the Valpolicella zone of the Veneto region in north-eastern Italy. It is made primarily from Corvina Veronese (45–95%), Corvinone (up to 50% as a substitute for Corvina), and Rondinella (5–30%) grapes, which are harvested in autumn and then laid on bamboo racks or in wooden crates in well-ventilated lofts for a minimum of 90 days — a technique known as apassimento. During this drying period the grapes lose 30–40% of their weight through water evaporation, concentrating sugars, polyphenols, and aromatic compounds. The dried grapes are then slowly fermented to near-dryness over several weeks, producing a wine with a typical alcohol content of 14–17% ABV and residual sugar below 4 g/L. The resulting wine displays an intense ruby-red colour with garnet nuances, an elegant bouquet of ripe plum, dried cherry, dark chocolate, tobacco, and spice, and a full-bodied palate with velvety, silken tannins and a long, harmonious finish. The Alfabeto label from Cantina Valpantena represents a cooperative-produced expression of this DOCG appellation. Nutritionally, Amarone is low in carbohydrates (approximately 2.6 g per 100 ml) and sugars (approximately 0.6 g per 100 ml), contains negligible fat and dietary fibre, and derives most of its caloric content (approximately 85 kcal per 100 ml) from ethanol. Its glycemic index is effectively zero, making it a low-GI beverage, though alcohol itself can affect blood glucose regulation. Clinically, the high alcohol content is the primary concern for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. Amarone is traditionally served at 16–18°C in large-bowled glasses and is classically paired with braised beef, game, lamb, aged hard cheeses such as Parmigiano-Reggiano, and rich pasta dishes. It is also consumed as a meditation wine on its own. The wine requires DOCG certification, guaranteeing geographic origin and production method compliance under Italian wine law.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is adapted from USDA SR Legacy generic red table wine (Food ID 11628) because no producer- or vintage-specific approved-source entry exists for Amarone della Valpolicella DOCG. Amarone typically has higher alcohol and slightly higher energy than standard table wine due to the apassimento process; values should be treated as indicative. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for red wine.
notes: fat_g is naturally zero for red wine as grapes and their fermented juice contain no lipid fraction. saturated_fat_g is naturally zero by the same reasoning. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as wine is a clarified liquid with no intact plant cell-wall material. cholesterol_mg is naturally zero as wine is a plant-derived product. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no significant glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Veritas sentinel convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate per 150 g serving is approximately 3.9 g and GI is effectively zero. monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_c_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for generic red wine. This is an Italian wine, not a Singaporean dish; multilingual local_names fields for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese are null as no mandatory multilingual naming requirement applies to Italian cuisine under Veritas v2 rules.
